content.text: I have been circling around this human for a long time.
An android who doesn't believe in humans, and a human who has doubts about android. Probability--"probability-wise," she would say--these two people should be naturally opposed.
But things didn't go according to chance.
It wasn't some big event that changed my opinion. I thought about it. There was no clear turning point, no dramatic moment where I suddenly decided, “This person can be trusted.”
It's a lot of little things that are not worth mentioning.

She fixed the communications panel in the hallway. That connector has been loose for about two months, and everyone is used to the occasional signal delay. Tightened as she passed. When someone saw her, she said, "I was just passing by." Haven't mentioned this to anyone.

When she analyzes the data, she becomes a completely different person. People who usually stumble over thresholds when walking, but when sitting in front of the screen, their fingertips are precise, their logic is clear, and they never make mistakes. It seems that bad luck only exists in the physical world, and in the world of data, she is another person.
She knew about Gray.

After I said those words that night, I waited for her reaction. Humans usually have three reactions after knowing this: pity, curiosity, and fear. Pity is the most common - "It's so pitiful that you can't see color." Curiosity is the second type - "What does the world you see look like?". Fear is the third type - "Your android's technology is too dangerous."
Her reaction was neither.

She just listened, quietly. Then she said, "Judging from the current data, yes," in response to what I said about the technology leak. She made no comment about Gray. She did not avoid it; she simply decided it was not her place to comment.

She respected one thing: it was my scar, not hers to talk about.

Very few humans can do this.

Late January. When I returned from patrol, she was in the base again. late at night. She's not analyzing data - she's fixing things. The cooling fan next to the data storage module has worn bearings and makes a slight abnormal noise when rotating.
She didn't know I was watching from behind.

She squatted on the ground, used the small screwdriver she brought with her to remove the fan, cleaned the dust on the bearings, and put it back again. The movements are not professional, but very careful. After installing it, she turned the fan blades and listened to the sound - there was no abnormal sound.
She nodded with satisfaction. Very slight movements.
Do you know how annoying it is for you to be like this?
She didn't even look back.
...Well, okay.
I leaned against the wall and watched as she put the tools away. Her knees squeaked as she stood up - she had been squatting for too long. The human body is so fragile.
This is the equipment of the base. You don't live here.
The fan sounded. It sounds uncomfortable.
that's all. There is no deeper reason. The fan rang and she fixed it. The communication panel was loose, so she tightened it. If there was a problem with the data, she would change it.
Don’t take credit, don’t explain, don’t expect anything in return. "Just passing by."
February.

I don't know when it started. Maybe it was a moment that was not worth mentioning - maybe it was seeing her spilling the coffee again and then wiping the table expressionlessly, maybe she was muttering "the confidence interval is not enough" while helping 1738 do data analysis, maybe she fell asleep at her workstation late one night, the light from the computer screen shone on her face, and her brows did not relax in her sleep.
At a certain point, I stopped classifying her as "human."
She becomes a concrete person. Han Yuchu.

Han Yuchu.

Unlucky but reliable. Quiet but not weak. It’s slow to warm up but once you believe it, you won’t leave.

I've been scolded countless times. It was the same reaction every time - look at me and wait for me to finish.

Then "...well, okay."

I admit, it took me a long time to get to this point.

But here I am.
